Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court

Party_Details.Name: Chen Songlin Michael

Facts.Text: On 2020-10-21, Michael Chen pleaded guilty to 13 offences under section 197 of the Companies Act 1967 relating to failures by nine companies to lodge annual returns with the Registrar of Companies on time.
As at 2020-10-21, Michael Chen was a nominee director in each of nine client companies of Eri Organisation Pte Ltd for which annual returns had not been lodged on time.
On 2020-10-21, Michael Chen was fined following his convictions under section 197 of the Companies Act 1967.
On 2020-10-21, Michael Chen became an automatic five-year disqualification period from acting as a director in any company under section 155 of the Companies Act 1967, running from 2020-10-21.
Immediately before 2020-10-21, Michael Chen was a director of Eri Organisation Pte Ltd, Eri Accounting Service Pte Ltd and Eri Secretarial Service Pte Ltd.

On 2020-10-21, Michael Chen ceased to be a director of Eri Organisation Pte Ltd, Eri Accounting Service Pte Ltd and Eri Secretarial Service Pte Ltd due to the commencement of his disqualification period.
As at 2020, Michael Chen was married and his wife held 20 per cent of the shares in Eri Organisation Pte Ltd.
As at 2020, Michael Chen held 80 per cent of the shares in Eri Organisation Pte Ltd and his wife held the remaining 20 per cent.
As at 2020, Eri Organisation Pte Ltd wholly owned Eri Accounting Service Pte Ltd and Eri Secretarial Service Pte Ltd.
In 2021, Michael Chen attended training courses on regulatory compliance and corporate secretarial practice.

On 2021-11-01, in HC/OS 785/2021, the High Court granted Michael Chen permission under section 155 of the Companies Act 1967 to act as a director of and to manage Eri Organisation Pte Ltd, Eri Accounting Service Pte Ltd and Eri Secretarial Service Pte Ltd.
On 2021-11-01, Michael Chen resumed acting as a director and manager of Eri Organisation Pte Ltd, Eri Accounting Service Pte Ltd and Eri Secretarial Service Pte Ltd pursuant to court permission.

By 2021-11-01, Michael Chen had overseen the implementation of systems within the Eri entities to monitor deadlines for clients to file annual returns and other submissions required under the Companies Act 1967.
By 2021-11-01, Michael Chen's wife, who was then the sole director of the Eri entities, was experiencing ill health and was no longer able to manage the Eri entities.
Before his disqualification in October 2020, the Eri entities had filed their annual returns in compliance with the Companies Act 1967.

From 2021-11-01 onwards, after Michael Chen obtained permission to act as a director of the Eri entities, the Eri entities and their clients complied with filing obligations under the Companies Act 1967, including annual returns.

Before 2020-10-21, when Michael Chen realised that the nine nominee client companies had not filed their annual returns on time, Eri Secretarial Service Pte Ltd sent reminders to those companies and their other directors urging them to remedy the non-compliance.
Before 2020-10-21, Michael Chen was unable to contact the foreign directors and shareholders of the nine nominee client companies regarding the late annual returns.

Before 2020-10-21, Eri Organisation Pte Ltd was unable to strike off the nine nominee client companies from the register due in part to restrictions on Michael Chen's access to those companies' accounts by the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority.
By 2020, Michael Chen acknowledged that he should have been more diligent in ensuring that the annual returns of the nine nominee client companies were filed on time.
On 2021-11-01, a minute sheet was created in HC/OS 785/2021 recording that the High Court granted Michael Chen permission to act as a director and manage the Eri entities.

By 2021-11-01, Michael Chen had filed an affidavit in HC/OS 785/2021 setting out his shareholding in the Eri entities, the nature of Eri's business, the circumstances of his convictions, and his mitigation, contrition and hardship.
In 2022-10-01, CEASY Tech Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore.
As at 2022-10-01, Michael Chen was the majority shareholder of CEASY Tech Pte Ltd.
As at 2022-10-01, CEASY Tech Pte Ltd had three directors: Bao Zehua, Zhang Shuhui and Chen Hao Rong.
By 2022-10-01, Bao Zehua had been appointed a director of Eri Accounting Service Pte Ltd and Zhang Shuhui had been appointed a director of Eri Secretarial Service Pte Ltd.
By 2023, Michael Chen had caused the appointments of two CEASY Tech Pte Ltd directors, Bao Zehua and Zhang Shuhui, to directorships in the Eri entities.
By 2023, Michael Chen had applied for a bank account for CEASY Tech Pte Ltd and was informed by a bank representative that there was a problem with one of the directors or shareholders.
By 2023, CEASY Tech Pte Ltd had not successfully opened a bank account, and Michael Chen attributed this to his disqualification status, although he did not have written confirmation from the bank.

In 2023, Michael Chen filed an affidavit in OA 561/2023 describing his shareholding in CEASY Tech Pte Ltd, its incorporation date, its directors and shareholders, and his reasons for seeking permission to act as a director or manager of CEASY Tech Pte Ltd.
On 2023-08-02, the High Court heard Michael Chen's application in OA 561/2023 for permission under section 155(1) of the Companies Act 1967 to act as a director of and/or manage CEASY Tech Pte Ltd.
By 2023-08-02, Michael Chen had served more than two years and seven months of his five-year disqualification period that commenced on 2020-10-21.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a person who is automatically disqualified under section 155(1) of the Companies Act 1967 due to persistent defaults in filing-related requirements should be granted court permission to act as a director of and/or take part in the management of a specific company, CEASY Tech Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: Under section 155(1) of the Companies Act 1967, a person who has been persistently in default of statutory filing and notification requirements is automatically disqualified for five years from acting as a director, promoter, or taking part in the management of a company, unless the court grants permission. In considering whether to grant permission, courts may assess factors such as: the applicant's capacity for future compliance with Companies Act requirements; any mitigating circumstances surrounding past non-compliance; the structure and nature of the company for which permission is sought; the interests of the public, shareholders, creditors and other stakeholders and whether these interests may be prejudiced; and whether it is necessary for the applicant to be involved in the management of the company. These factors are applied holistically to balance the applicant's and company's interest in productive activity against the regulatory interest in protecting companies, stakeholders and the public.
Party_Details.Application: Michael Chen was automatically disqualified for five years from 2020-10-21 due to multiple offences under section 197 of the Companies Act 1967 relating to late filing of annual returns by nine nominee client companies for which he was a director. Before his disqualification, the Eri entities that he controlled had complied with filing obligations. After his disqualification, he ceased to be a director of the Eri entities, and his wife, who held 20 per cent of Eri Organisation Pte Ltd and was then the sole director of the Eri entities, experienced ill health and was no longer able to manage them. In 2021, he attended regulatory compliance and corporate secretarial training courses and implemented systems within the Eri entities to monitor filing deadlines. From November 2021 onwards, after obtaining court permission in HC/OS 785/2021 to act as a director of the Eri entities, those entities and their clients complied with Companies Act filing obligations. The circumstances leading to his convictions included late filings by nine nominee client companies where Eri Secretarial Service Pte Ltd had sent reminders and attempted to contact foreign directors and shareholders but received no responses and faced restrictions on access to company accounts, limiting the ability to strike off those companies. CEASY Tech Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore in October 2022 as an exempt private company limited by shares. Michael Chen is its majority shareholder, and the company has three directors, two of whom also serve as directors in the Eri entities and were appointed to those roles through Michael Chen's actions. As majority shareholder, Michael Chen can influence CEASY Tech Pte Ltd indirectly through its directors. He applied for a bank account for CEASY Tech Pte Ltd and was informed verbally that there was a problem with one of the directors or shareholders, and he attributed this to his disqualification, although he did not obtain written confirmation. By the time of his 2023 application in OA 561/2023, he had served more than two years and seven months of his five-year disqualification period. The Attorney-General, representing the Minister for Finance, did not oppose his application and accepted that the court could consider the factual background from HC/OS 785/2021 when assessing his request for permission to act as a director of and/or manage CEASY Tech Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge identified that section 155(1) of the Companies Act 1967 imposes an automatic disqualification on persons who are persistently in default of filing and notification requirements, and that the statutory objective of this provision is predominantly protective, similar to section 154 but distinct from section 155A. The judgment traced the legislative history and policy concerns about directors of active companies failing to hold annual general meetings and file annual returns, and noted that such conduct prejudices stakeholders who rely on corporate disclosures. The judge compared the Singapore regime with the UK Company Directors Disqualification Act 1986 and cited authorities such as Re Haeusler, Ong Chow Hong, Derrick Chong, Huang Sheng Chang and Kardachi to explain how courts have approached applications for permission under sections 154(6) and 155A(3). From these authorities, the judge distilled a non-exhaustive framework for section 155 applications, listing five factors: (a) the applicant's capacity for future compliance with Companies Act requirements; (b) mitigating circumstances for past non-compliance; (c) the structure and nature of the company for which permission is sought; (d) the interests of the public, shareholders, creditors and other stakeholders and the risks to them; and (e) whether it is necessary for the applicant to be granted permission. The judge emphasised that these factors must be assessed holistically to balance the applicant's and company's interest in economically productive activity against the regulatory interest in protecting companies, stakeholders and the public from the prima facie risk of harm arising from the applicant's persistent defaults. Applying this framework, the judge evaluated Michael Chen's past conduct and found that, although his earlier defaults in relation to nine nominee companies did not initially indicate a high capacity for compliance, there were mitigating circumstances: he had caused reminders to be sent, attempted to contact foreign directors and shareholders, and faced difficulties such as restricted access to company accounts that impeded striking off the companies. The judge noted that Michael Chen acknowledged he should have been more diligent. The judge also relied on the fact that the Eri entities had historically complied with filing obligations, that Michael Chen had undertaken compliance training in 2021, and that after he obtained permission in HC/OS 785/2021, the Eri entities and their clients remained compliant with Companies Act filing requirements. These matters were treated as evidence of improved capacity for future compliance. On the structure and nature of CEASY Tech Pte Ltd, the judge observed that it is an exempt private company limited by shares, not subject to the more onerous disclosure regime applicable to public companies. The judge noted that Michael Chen is the majority shareholder and that two of CEASY's three directors also serve as directors in the Eri entities due to his actions, indicating that he already wields significant indirect influence over CEASY's management. The judge accepted the Attorney-General's submission that granting permission would allow Michael Chen to be held directly accountable for compliance by CEASY, while cautioning that indirect influence by a shareholder is only one factor and does not automatically justify permission in every case. In assessing stakeholder interests, the judge concluded that the risk to the public, CEASY, its shareholders and other stakeholders was low, given Michael Chen's demonstrated compliance since 2021 and the absence of opposition from the Minister. The judge considered Michael Chen's argument that his involvement was necessary because CEASY had difficulty opening a bank account, but found this unpersuasive due to the lack of documentary evidence from the bank and the fact that CEASY already had three directors who could potentially open an account. The judge therefore treated necessity as a neutral or weak factor. Weighing all factors, including that Michael Chen had already served more than half of his five-year disqualification period, the judge held that the protective purpose of section 155 would not be undermined by granting permission and that the balance of considerations favoured allowing Michael Chen to act as a director of and/or take part in the management of CEASY Tech Pte Ltd. The court accordingly allowed the application and granted the requested permission.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court

Party_Details.Name: Attorney-General

Facts.Text: On 2021-11-01, in HC/OS 785/2021, the Minister for Finance, represented by the Attorney-General, did not oppose Michael Chen's application for permission to act as a director of and manage the Eri entities under section 155 of the Companies Act 1967.

In 2023, in OA 561/2023, the Minister for Finance, represented by the Attorney-General, did not object to Michael Chen's application for permission to act as a director of and/or manage CEASY Tech Pte Ltd under section 155 of the Companies Act 1967.

In OA 561/2023, the Attorney-General accepted that the court could rely on the factual matters set out in HC/OS 785/2021 when considering Michael Chen's application for permission regarding CEASY Tech Pte Ltd.

In written submissions filed in OA 561/2023, the Attorney-General stated that Michael Chen had demonstrated capacity for compliance with Companies Act filing requirements since being granted permission to act as a director of the Eri entities in November 2021.

In written submissions filed in OA 561/2023, the Attorney-General noted that by the time of filing the application, Michael Chen had served more than two years and seven months of his five-year disqualification period under section 155 of the Companies Act 1967.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in light of the statutory purpose of section 155(1) of the Companies Act 1967 and the applicant's history of defaults and subsequent conduct, the Attorney-General should oppose or not oppose the applicant's request for court permission to act as a director of and/or manage CEASY Tech Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: When responding to an application under section 155(1) of the Companies Act 1967 for permission to act as a director or to take part in the management of a company, the Attorney-General may consider the statutory objective of the disqualification regime, which is to protect the public and corporate stakeholders from risks posed by individuals with a history of persistent defaults. Relevant considerations include the applicant's demonstrated capacity for future compliance, any mitigating circumstances surrounding past non-compliance, the nature and structure of the company for which permission is sought, the potential impact on stakeholders, and the period of disqualification already served. The Attorney-General may indicate whether there is any objection to the application based on these considerations.
Party_Details.Application: In HC/OS 785/2021, the Attorney-General, acting for the Minister for Finance, did not oppose Michael Chen's application for permission to act as a director of and manage the Eri entities after his automatic disqualification under section 155(1) of the Companies Act 1967. In OA 561/2023, the Attorney-General again did not object to Michael Chen's application for permission to act as a director of and/or manage CEASY Tech Pte Ltd. The Attorney-General accepted that the court could consider the factual background from HC/OS 785/2021, including Michael Chen's prior compliance record with the Eri entities, his attendance at compliance training, and the implementation of systems to monitor filing deadlines. In written submissions, the Attorney-General acknowledged that since November 2021, the Eri entities and their clients had complied with Companies Act filing obligations and that Michael Chen had served more than two years and seven months of his five-year disqualification period. The Attorney-General also submitted that, given Michael Chen's majority shareholding in CEASY Tech Pte Ltd and his influence over two of its directors who also serve as directors in the Eri entities, granting permission would allow him to be held directly accountable for compliance by CEASY Tech Pte Ltd. Taking these factors into account, the Attorney-General did not advance any argument that granting permission would undermine the protective purpose of section 155(1) or pose an unacceptable risk to the public or stakeholders of CEASY Tech Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge noted that the Attorney-General, representing the Minister for Finance, did not oppose Michael Chen's applications in either HC/OS 785/2021 or OA 561/2023. In the reasoning, the judge referred to the Attorney-General's acceptance that the factual background from HC/OS 785/2021 was relevant to the present application and to the Attorney-General's submissions that Michael Chen had demonstrated capacity for compliance since being granted permission to act as a director of the Eri entities. The judge also recorded the Attorney-General's position that Michael Chen's majority shareholding and influence over CEASY Tech Pte Ltd meant that granting permission would allow him to be held directly accountable for any future non-compliance, which was treated as a factor supporting the application. The absence of opposition from the Attorney-General was one of the contextual elements considered in the holistic assessment of whether the protective purpose of section 155(1) would be maintained while allowing Michael Chen to resume certain management functions.
